summary:
Sneferu was the founding pharaoh of the Fourth Dynasty of Egypt, reigning approximately 2613–2589 BC, and is credited with constructing more pyramid volume than any other ancient Egyptian ruler. He built at least three major pyramids — the Meidum Pyramid, the Bent Pyramid at Dahshur, and the Red Pyramid at Dahshur — in a sequence that appears to represent an experimental evolution of pyramid design. The Meidum structure began as a stepped pyramid and was later encased to create a smooth-sided form; the Bent Pyramid exhibits a change in slope partway up, suggesting the builders altered the angle mid-construction, possibly for structural reasons; and the Red Pyramid achieved a stable true pyramid form for the first time.

Sneferu's architectural experimentation laid the technical and organizational groundwork for the great monuments built by his successors. His son Khufu went on to commission the [[event:1708|Great Pyramid of Giza]], the most famous of all ancient Egyptian monuments. Sneferu is regarded by later Egyptian tradition as a benevolent and capable ruler, and the scale of his building program implies a highly organized state capable of mobilizing enormous labor forces and material resources across multiple simultaneous construction projects.
